result.markdown:
--- title: "Choosing the Cell Resolution" jupyter: python3 --- ```{=html} ``` # The resolution problem The scale-decomposition bound on preferential-sampling bias (Prop. *scale-decomposition* of the methodology) reads, on an equal-measure partition $\mathcal{P}_K$ with $K=K_{\mathrm{lon}}K_{\mathrm{lat}}K_t$, $$ \big|B_{\mathrm{samp}}\big| \;\le\;\sigma_{w^{\star}}\,\sigma_{Y^{\star}} \;+\;\sigma_{w^{\perp}}\,\sigma_{Y^{\perp}} , $$ with field factors $\sigma_{Y^\star}=\|y^\star\|_{L^2(\nu)}$ (resolved, between-cell) and $\sigma_{Y^\perp}=\|y^\perp\|_{L^2(\nu)}$ (within-cell), where $y^\star=\mathsf{P}_K(y-\mu)$, $y^\perp=\mathsf{P}_K^{\perp}(y-\mu)$ are the cell-averaging / within-cell projections of the OHC field $y\in L^2(\nu)$ and $\nu$ is the normalized area$\times$time reference measure on $\mathcal{X}=A\times\mathcal{T}$. The one factor that cannot be estimated from a sparse point pattern, $\sigma_{w^\perp}$, enters only through the product $\sigma_{w^\perp}\sigma_{Y^\perp}$; it is therefore bias-irrelevant to the extent the field is constant within cells. **So the cell scale must make the fraction of field variance below it, $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$, small** — while keeping the mean occupancy $N/K$ (in practice $F/K$, with $F$ the number of distinct floats) above one. The field factors, unlike $\sigma_{w^\star}$, are computable directly on the dense GLORYS field over the NAC box (lon $[-68,-62]$, lat $[36,40]$, 2020–2022 daily at $1/12^\circ$), which resolves $\sigma_{Y^\star},\sigma_{Y^\perp},\sigma_Y$ exactly. This report fixes the resolution in two logically separate steps — **spatial** ($K_{\mathrm{lon}},K_{\mathrm{lat}}$) and **temporal** ($K_t$) — and, importantly, the spatial choice can be made *independently of seasonality* (see below), so we settle it first. ::: {#setup .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code code-summary="Setup — run (or load cached) R analyses"} import sys, os sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code")) import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import resolution as R DEPTHS = ["700", "2000"] D = "../data/leakage" TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV = f"{D}/leakage_time.csv", f"{D}/leakage_full.csv", f"{D}/leakage_spatial.csv" VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF = f"{D}/variogram_ranges.csv", f"{D}/variogram_curves.csv", f"{D}/variogram_acf.csv" ARGO_CSV, OCC_CSV, SWEEP_CSV = f"{D}/argo_compare.csv", f"{D}/argo_occupancy.csv", f"{D}/argo_sweep.csv" if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV)): R.run_leakage_curves(TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV, depths=DEPTHS) if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF)): R.run_variogram(VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF, depths=DEPTHS) if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (ARGO_CSV, OCC_CSV, SWEEP_CSV)): R.run_argo_comparison(ARGO_CSV, OCC_CSV, SWEEP_CSV, depths=DEPTHS) time_df, full_df, spatial_df = R.load_tables(TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV) vg_ranges, vg_curves, vg_acf = R.load_tables(VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF) argo_df, occ_df, sweep_df = R.load_tables(ARGO_CSV, OCC_CSV, SWEEP_CSV) ``` ::: # Spatial resolution {#sec-spatial} ::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"} **This section is free of seasonality.** The quantities below are *per-snapshot spatial* variances. A seasonal cycle that is (near) spatially uniform shifts every location on a given day by the same amount, and an additive constant does not change that day's spatial variance. So the spatial within-cell variance — and hence the choice of $K_{\mathrm{lon}},K_{\mathrm{lat}}$ — is settled on raw OHC without any deseasonalizing. Seasonality enters only the temporal scale, handled separately in @sec-temporal. ::: The most direct test of a spatial grid is empirical: partition each daily snapshot into $K_{\mathrm{lon}}\times K_{\mathrm{lat}}$ equal-measure (lon $\times \sin$lat) cells, take the $\cos(\mathrm{lat})$ area-weighted within-cell spatial variance and total spatial variance, and average over snapshots (`spatial_leakage` in `code/leakage_curve.R`, eq. *sigmaYperp-hat* evaluated on the native $1/12^\circ$ grid). Their ratio is the **fraction of spatial field variance below the cell scale**, $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$ — exactly the quantity the bound needs small. As cells shrink it must fall to zero; the question is how small it already is at the coarsest cell the floats can support, $s_F\approx 76$ km. ::: {#cell-spatial-fraction .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ```
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](resolution_files/figure-html/spatial-fraction-output-2.png){#spatial-fraction width=662 height=431} ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ```
``` ::: ::: ::: {#spatial-table .cell execution_count=4}
depth target_km K_lon K_lat n_cells cell (km) var frac (perp^2/Y^2) sd ratio (perp/Y)
0-700 m 200 3 2 6 197.069 0.360 0.600
0-700 m 150 3 3 9 160.906 0.266 0.515
0-700 m 100 5 4 20 107.939 0.157 0.396
0-700 m 75 7 6 42 74.485 0.086 0.293
0-700 m 50 10 9 90 50.883 0.044 0.210
0-700 m 35 15 13 195 34.568 0.021 0.147
0-700 m 25 21 18 378 24.828 0.011 0.105
0-700 m 18 29 25 725 17.928 0.005 0.072
0-700 m 12 44 37 1628 11.964 0.002 0.043
0-2000 m 200 3 2 6 197.069 0.324 0.570
0-2000 m 150 3 3 9 160.906 0.238 0.487
0-2000 m 100 5 4 20 107.939 0.135 0.367
0-2000 m 75 7 6 42 74.485 0.072 0.268
0-2000 m 50 10 9 90 50.883 0.036 0.189
0-2000 m 35 15 13 195 34.568 0.017 0.131
0-2000 m 25 21 18 378 24.828 0.009 0.093
0-2000 m 18 29 25 725 17.928 0.004 0.064
0-2000 m 12 44 37 1628 11.964 0.001 0.038
::: Reading off the curve at $s_F\approx76$ km fixes the spatial grid: the smallest $K_{\mathrm{lon}}\times K_{\mathrm{lat}}$ whose cell size sits at or below the decorrelation scale while keeping the residual fraction acceptably small. The paper's operating grid $K_{\mathrm{lon}}=6,\ K_{\mathrm{lat}}=5$ ($\Delta x\approx88$ km, $\Delta y\approx89$ km) is read directly from this curve. ## Decorrelation length (directional variogram) The variogram corroborates the cell-size curve above and, unlike it, exposes the **anisotropy** that the front imposes. Within each snapshot we form grid-point pairs (subsampled), classify each by orientation, and accumulate the semivariance $\tfrac12(y_i-y_j)^2$ into zonal ($|\theta|<30^\circ$), meridional ($|\theta|>60^\circ$) and omnidirectional bins, pooled over snapshots — again a per-snapshot construction, so again seasonality-free. It *must* be directional: correlation is long along the current and short across it, and an isotropic average would hide the short across-front scale, which is the binding one. (Raw OHC has a per-snapshot planar trend removed so the standing Gulf-Stream gradient does not render the variogram non-stationary; the deseasonalized panel removes the per-snapshot mean.) ::: {#variograms .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.panel-tabset} ### 0-700 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/variograms-output-2.png){#variograms-1 width=1143 height=418}
### 0-2000 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/variograms-output-4.png){#variograms-2 width=1143 height=418}
::: ::: The binding relevance scale is the **shorter, meridional (across-front)** range: $\sigma_{Y^\perp}$ is controlled by field variation in *every* within-cell direction, so the shortest one governs. The methodology's verdict is $L_{\mathrm{rel}}\gtrsim s_F=\sqrt{|A|/F}\approx76$ km (eq. *verdict*): the field's shortest decorrelation length must be no finer than the scale the floats can resolve. Where it holds, cells at the sampling scale sit within a decorrelation length and $\sigma_{Y^\perp}$ is small; where it fails, a band of bias-relevant structure is unresolved and the residual term must be carried rather than assumed away. ::: {#ranges-table .cell execution_count=6}
depth field direction sill range 1/e (km) range exp (km)
0-700 m raw omni 43.513 85.2 69.4
0-700 m raw zonal 43.513 100.0 118.1
0-700 m raw merid 43.513 76.1 50.3
0-700 m deseas omni 28.389 80.4 67.8
0-700 m deseas zonal 28.389 84.5 88.6
0-700 m deseas merid 28.389 76.3 51.8
0-2000 m raw omni 90.477 89.0 72.7
0-2000 m raw zonal 90.477 101.5 119.0
0-2000 m raw merid 90.477 81.3 53.7
0-2000 m deseas omni 59.914 87.6 76.7
0-2000 m deseas zonal 59.914 88.9 97.2
0-2000 m deseas merid 59.914 86.5 60.2
::: # Temporal resolution and seasonality {#sec-temporal} ::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"} **This section is where seasonality lives.** The number of time bins $K_t$ is governed by temporal structure of the regional-mean OHC series, which is dominated by the seasonal cycle; the spatial choice of @sec-spatial does not depend on any of it. ::: Collapsing space to the area-weighted regional mean $\bar Y(t)$ (the $K_{\mathrm{lon}}=K_{\mathrm{lat}}=1$ special case of $\mathcal{P}_K$), we bin the 2020–2022 series into $K_t$ equal-duration bins and report the fraction of its temporal variance left within bins, $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$, for the **raw** series and for a **deseasonalized** series (minus a per-cell, per-calendar-month GLORYS climatology — an Argo-independent baseline). The gap between them is the seasonal cycle: the raw fraction stays large until many time bins resolve the seasonal swing, whereas deseasonalizing removes it up front so even a coarse $K_t$ leaks little. ::: {#temporal-fraction .cell execution_count=7} ::: {.panel-tabset} ## 0-700 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/temporal-fraction-output-2.png){#temporal-fraction-1 width=664 height=431}
## 0-2000 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/temporal-fraction-output-4.png){#temporal-fraction-2 width=664 height=431}
::: ::: The lagged autocorrelation of the deseasonalized regional mean gives the residual (non-seasonal) temporal decorrelation scale; its $e$-folding lag, together with the seasonal amplitude above, sets $K_t$. ::: {#cell-temporal-acf .cell execution_count=8} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ```
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](resolution_files/figure-html/temporal-acf-output-2.png){#temporal-acf width=645 height=412} ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ```
``` ::: ::: ::: {#temporal-table .cell execution_count=9}
depth K_t raw deseas
0-700 m 3 0.968 0.941
0-700 m 6 0.528 0.728
0-700 m 12 0.483 0.657
0-700 m 24 0.225 0.327
0-2000 m 3 0.936 0.905
0-2000 m 6 0.608 0.733
0-2000 m 12 0.564 0.668
0-2000 m 24 0.294 0.363
::: # Operating partition {#sec-operating} On the full partition $\mathcal{P}_K$ at the operating spatial grid ($K_{\mathrm{lon}}=6,\ K_{\mathrm{lat}}=5$), the combined field variance splits by the Pythagorean identity $\sigma_Y^2=\sigma_{Y^\star}^2+\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2$ (eq. *sigmaYK-hat / sigmaYperp-hat*, native grid aggregated to equal-measure cells). The residual diagnostic there, across $K_t$: ::: {#operating-checks .cell execution_count=10} Pythagorean identity holds by construction (max residual $2.8e-14$); the equal-measure quadrature reconstructs the direct $\cos(\mathrm{lat})$-weighted field variance to within **0.8%** (largest relative gap across resolutions), bounding the error of treating the discrete cells as exactly equal-measure. Combined space$\times$time variance fraction $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$:
depth K_t raw deseas
0-700 m 3 0.332 0.965
0-700 m 6 0.308 0.918
0-700 m 12 0.276 0.826
0-700 m 24 0.224 0.682
0-2000 m 3 0.303 0.957
0-2000 m 6 0.285 0.913
0-2000 m 12 0.251 0.805
0-2000 m 24 0.199 0.648
::: # Observed Argo profiles vs GLORYS truth {#sec-argo} Everything above is computed on the dense GLORYS field, i.e. against the reference measure $\nu$. The real Argo profiles instead realize the *sampling* measure $\pi$: their spatial–temporal locations are a point pattern with intensity proportional to $\pi$, and their OHC is the field sampled at those points. Using the observed profile OHC (`data/argo_ohc.csv`, restricted to the same NAC box and 2020–2022) we can (i) measure how uneven the sampling actually is — the between-cell weight dispersion $\sigma_{w^\star}$ that the GLORYS field alone cannot supply — and (ii) check whether the sparse, clustered sample *recovers* the field's within/between-cell OHC structure or distorts it, as preferential sampling would. This is the empirical counterpart of the bias bound: it pairs the observed $\sigma_{w^\star}$ with the GLORYS $\sigma_{Y^\star}$. ::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"} **Depth reliability.** In this box $99\%$ of profiles reach $700$ m but only $\sim\!5\%$ reach $2000$ m (median profile depth $\approx 1980$ m). The Argo $0$–$2000$ m OHC is therefore integrated over a truncated, profile-dependent column: this biases its *mean* low and inflates its *within-cell variance* independently of sampling. **The $0$–$700$ m comparison is the clean one and we read the conclusions from it;** $0$–$2000$ m is shown for completeness under this caveat. ::: ## Sampling weights (occupancy) On the operating grid, occupancy estimates the cell sampling probability $\pi_j = n_j/N$ and hence the between-cell weight $\bar w_j = K\pi_j$ ($\bar w_j = 1$ is uniform, $\nu$-matched sampling). Its dispersion is the resolved sampling factor $\sigma_{w^\star}^2 = \tfrac1K\sum_j(\bar w_j-1)^2$ (eq. *sigma-w-parts*). ::: {#argo-occupancy .cell execution_count=11} ### 0-700 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/argo-occupancy-output-2.png){#argo-occupancy-1 width=675 height=412}
### 0-2000 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/argo-occupancy-output-4.png){#argo-occupancy-2 width=675 height=412}
::: The profiles keep all $K_{\mathrm{lon}}\times K_{\mathrm{lat}}=30$ operating cells populated ($\geq 5$ profiles each), with a moderate resolved dispersion $\sigma_{w^\star}\approx 0.34$. Occupancy is the binding constraint on how fine the grid can go: as cells shrink past the float floor $s_F$, cells empty out and the within-cell terms become inestimable.
![](resolution_files/figure-html/argo-sweep-output-2.png){#argo-sweep width=660 height=431}
## Within- and between-cell OHC: Argo vs GLORYS For each $K_t$ the profiles are binned into the same equal-measure $\mathcal{P}_K$ cells; cell means and within-cell variances are taken over the profiles (unweighted — the sample already carries the sampling intensity), and the field factors use equal cell weight $1/K$. Solid lines are the GLORYS truth (reference measure); open markers the Argo sample. ::: {#argo-compare .cell execution_count=13} ### 0-700 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/argo-compare-output-2.png){#argo-compare-1 width=1047 height=419}
### 0-2000 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/argo-compare-output-4.png){#argo-compare-2 width=1047 height=419}
::: At $0$–$700$ m the sparse sample recovers the field structure well: the between-cell factor $\sigma_{Y^\star}$ and the within-cell fraction $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$ track the GLORYS truth closely, with the Argo $\sigma_{Y^\perp}$ mildly inflated (few profiles per cell, and profiles in a cell span different days within the time bin add temporal spread to the "spatial" within-cell term). At $0$–$2000$ m the Argo within-cell fraction is visibly inflated relative to truth — the variable profile-depth truncation flagged above, not a property of the field. ## Realized bias and the design-based cap The naive profile average $\hat\mu_\pi = \tfrac1N\sum_i y_i$ is the $\pi$-mean; its gap from the GLORYS $\nu$-mean $\mu$ is the *realized* discrepancy, while the cell-averaged estimator $\hat\mu_{\mathrm{grid}} = \tfrac1K\sum_j\bar y_j$ partially corrects the between-cell oversampling. The design-based **between-cell cap** $\sigma_{w^\star}\sigma_{Y^\star}$ (observed $\sigma_{w^\star}$, truth $\sigma_{Y^\star}$) bounds the *preferential-sampling* part of the bias. ::: {#argo-bias .cell execution_count=14}
depth K_t sigma_w* sigma_Y* (GJ) cap sigma_w*sigma_Y* (GJ) |bias| naive (GJ) |bias| grid (GJ)
0-700 m 1 0.343 8.80 3.02 2.74 2.30
0-700 m 3 0.598 8.86 5.29 2.74 1.88
0-700 m 6 0.852 9.02 7.68 2.74 2.50
0-700 m 12 1.136 9.22 10.48 2.74 2.40
0-2000 m 1 0.342 13.65 4.67 7.39 6.80
0-2000 m 3 0.593 13.74 8.15 7.39 5.99
0-2000 m 6 0.847 13.92 11.79 7.39 6.92
0-2000 m 12 1.132 14.24 16.13 7.39 6.81
At $0$–$700$ m the naive profile mean underestimates the GLORYS truth by **2.74 GJ m$^{-2}$** ($-6.0\%$ of $\mu=46.0$), and cell-averaging trims this to 2.30. Even the tightest, pure-spatial cap ($K_t=1$) $\sigma_{w^\star}\sigma_{Y^\star}=3.02$ GJ m$^{-2}$ contains it — whereas at $0$–$2000$ m the truncation-inflated gap exceeds that cap, confirming it is not sampling alone. ::: ::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"} **What this gap does and does not isolate.** The realized naive/grid discrepancy conflates preferential sampling with any systematic GLORYS-vs-observation offset (reanalysis mean-state bias, representativeness) — and at $0$–$2000$ m with the depth truncation above, which is why the $2000$ m $|$bias$|$ can exceed the preferential-sampling cap. Only the design-based product $\sigma_{w^\star}\sigma_{Y^\star}$ (plus the within-cell term) isolates the sampling contribution as a worst case. The clean sampling-only bias — GLORYS co-located at the float positions, same field on both sides — is the subject of the main sampling-bias experiment; this section is an independent check with the real profiles that the sampling is preferential ($\sigma_{w^\star}>0$) and that the field structure the bound relies on is recoverable from the observed sample. :::
